narrative:
Los futuros de los principales índices estadounidenses muestran una apertura al alza, con el S&P 500 recuperando gran parte de las pérdidas sufridas durante el conflicto en Medio Oriente. Este movimiento refleja un optimismo creciente entre los inversionistas, impulsado por la expectativa de que el alto al fuego en la guerra con Irán se mantenga y que las negociaciones de paz se reanuden pronto. La posibilidad de un fin cercano al conflicto reduce la percepción de riesgo, lo que favorece un rebote en las acciones, aunque la cautela persiste debido a la volatilidad que aún puede mantenerse.

En el mercado, las acciones tecnológicas y financieras lideran las ganancias, apoyadas por resultados trimestrales sólidos que muestran resiliencia en las ganancias corporativas. Por otro lado, los precios del petróleo Brent se mantienen elevados, cerca de los 90 dólares por barril, debido a la interrupción del comercio marítimo hacia Irán y las preocupaciones sobre el suministro energético. Los bonos del Tesoro muestran rendimientos estables, reflejando que los inversionistas institucionales aún valoran la incertidumbre geopolítica, mientras que el mercado de metales preciosos registra caídas moderadas.

Desde la perspectiva institucional, los grandes fondos y bancos están aumentando su exposición a acciones, especialmente en sectores tecnológicos y financieros, confiando en que la guerra no escalará nuevamente en el corto plazo. Sin embargo, mantienen una postura neutral en el corto plazo debido a que las valoraciones ya se acercan a niveles previos al conflicto y la volatilidad podría persistir si las negociaciones de paz enfrentan obstáculos. La persistencia de precios altos en la energía también genera expectativas de que la Reserva Federal no reducirá las tasas de interés pronto, a pesar de cierta desaceleración en el aumento de precios al productor.

El escenario podría cambiar rápidamente si el alto al fuego se rompe o si las conversaciones entre Estados Unidos e Irán fracasan, lo que reavivaría las tensiones y aumentaría la incertidumbre en los mercados. Además, la publicación de datos económicos clave esta semana, como las ventas minoristas en Estados Unidos, el informe Beige Book de la Fed y los resultados trimestrales de grandes bancos y empresas tecnológicas, serán vigilados de cerca para ajustar las expectativas sobre el crecimiento económico y la política monetaria. En este contexto, los inversionistas deben estar atentos a cualquier señal que modifique la percepción de estabilidad en Medio Oriente o que impacte la evolución de los precios energéticos.
regime:
🏷️ MARKET REGIME: Régimen Transicional / Volátil 🟡

El mercado muestra una recuperación técnica tras un fuerte rebote desde mínimos recientes, con compras presentes pero sin impulso claro para romper resistencias preexistentes. Las rupturas alcistas se mantienen, pero la continuidad es débil y la volatilidad sigue elevada, reflejando incertidumbre sobre la sostenibilidad del avance. Típicamente, en este tipo de régimen, los traders deben esperar movimientos erráticos y reaccionar con cautela ante noticias, ya que la reacción del mercado es mixta y la consistencia del movimiento limitada. En situaciones como esta, es recomendable priorizar la gestión del riesgo y evitar posiciones excesivamente direccionales.
market_drivers:
● Futuros del S&P 500 recuperan pérdidas y se acercan a máximos preconflicto, reflejando optimismo por posible fin de guerra en Irán.  
● Aixtron ($AIXG) sube tras elevar guía por fuerte demanda en equipos optoelectrónicos, impulsando sector tecnología europeo.  
● ASML ($ASML) reporta ganancias mejores a lo esperado y aumenta previsión anual, apoyando acciones de semiconductores.  
● Stellantis ($STLA) avanza tras reportar crecimiento sólido en envíos Q1, beneficiando sector automotriz global.  
● Hermes ($RMS) cae por ventas Q1 por debajo de expectativas, afectando acciones de lujo y consumo discrecional.  
● Kering ($KER) retrocede tras reporte de ventas planas y debilidad en Gucci, presionando segmento de lujo europeo.  
● BlackRock incrementa exposición en tecnología y finanzas, señalando confianza institucional en sectores con potencial de recuperación.  
● La posible extensión del alto al fuego en Medio Oriente reduce riesgo geopolítico, favoreciendo apetito por activos de riesgo.

vital.source_subject: Vital Knowledge: Vital Dawn for Wednesday April 15, 2026
vital.market_levels:
Market levels – the US equity futures indices are flattish so far this morning: S&P +2 points/3bp, Dow -29 points/6bp, Nasdaq +20 points/8bp, and R2 K -1.9 points/7bp. The major Eurozone indices are mixed , with the SX5 E falling 15bp while the SXXP inches up 5bp. There’s outperformance in Europe in autos, basic resources, tech, media, real estate, and healthcare while utilities, energy, telecoms, insurance, banks, and luxury underperform . Aixtron and ASML are both climbing on earnings ( Aixton is the top stock in the SXXP) while Hermes and Kering get hit hard following their results. Stellantis is climbing on solid Q1 shipment numbers. Stocks in Asia mostly saw gains on Wed: TPX +0.4%, NKY +0.44%, Hang Seng +0.29%, HSCEI +0.54%, SHCOMP +0.01%, Shenzhen -0.71%, Taiwan TAIEX +1.17%, Korea KOSPI +2.07%, Australia ASX 200 +0.09%, and India is up ~1.6%. Treasuries are seeing modest weakness so far this morning (yields are flat-to-up 2bp) and Fed expectations aren’t moving much (the market is pricing in 10bp of Fed cuts this year). The DXY is about flat. Brent is climbing 60bp to ~$95.35 while gold falls 75bp and silver drops 90bp. Bitcoin is inching down 15bp (to ~$74 K).
•
vital.whats_happening:
What’s happening this morning – it’s fairly quiet as markets wait for the next development out of Iran . Trump sprayed the news wires with a lot of hopeful rhetoric during a trio of interviews on Tues (NY Post, Fox, and ABC) and myriad media outlets are talking about another face-to-face meeting within days, but (so far) nothing formal has been scheduled. It’s slowly becoming the consensus that the war (at least the military component of it) has peaked , with a sustained détente now the base case assumption (that’s our perspective too, but the removal of skepticism creates a downside risk for markets to the extent any hiccups emerge ). A Washington Post article around 5am ET contained the somewhat ominous headline “US sends thousands more troops to Mideast” , although that framing is a bit misleading (the troops are on boats that have been traveling to the region for several days, and so this isn’t as incremental as it may seem ); all the tea leaves still suggest Trump is pivoting aggressively away from kinetic escalation . There wasn’t anything major on the economic data or monetary policy fronts since the Tues close. For earnings , the big developments are European luxury , where both Hermes and Kering are getting hit following underwhelming Q1 sales results, and European tech , where ASML and Aixtron rally on back of solid results/guidance.
•
vital.watching_next:
What we’ll be watching on Wednesday – the focus will be on earnings before the US open ( BAC, PNC , and MS ), US import/export prices for Mar at 8:30am ET, the Fed’s Beige Book survey at 2pm ET, earnings after the US close ( SLG ), and China’s retail sales/industrial production for Mar and GDP for Q1 Wed night.
• What we’ll be watching on Thursday – the focus will be on earnings after the Asia close ( TSM ), earnings before the US open ( ABT, BK, MRSH, PEP, PLD, SCHW, TRV , and USB ), and earnings after the US close ( AA and NFLX ).
• What we’ll be watching on Friday – the focus will be on earnings before the US open ( ALLY, FITB, RF, STT , and TFC ).
• Market in a Minute (macro) – Trump told Fox’s Maria Bartiromo in an interview airing Wed morning that the war is “very close to being over” and mentioned to ABC News that “ I think you’re going to be watching an amazing two days ahead ” while saying in a NY Post interview that additional peace negotiations “could be happening over the next two days” ( Fox , NY Post , Bloomberg ); the Pentagon is moving thousands of additional troops into the Middle East ( Washington Post ); Bessent on Tues said US tariffs could be back to where they stood before the SCOTUS decision by July ( Bloomberg ); Europe is preparing a NATO replacement in case Trump winds up exiting the US from the alliance ( WSJ ); NY Gov Hochul has proposed an annual tax on second homes in NYC worth $5 M+ ( NYT )
• Market in a Minute (micro – Wednesday morning) - Aixtron shares spike in European trading after the company preannounced upside on Q1 orders (EU171 MM vs. the Street EU134 MM) and raised its full-year guidance for sales and EBIT margins due to stronger than expected optoelectronics equipment demand ( Aixtron ); ARES (Ares)’s co-founder and CEO said credit quality is relatively healthy in the private credit industry ( Reuters ); ASML (ASML Holding) reported solid upside on Q1 EPS (EU7.15 vs. the Street EU6.65) thanks to strength in sales and margins, and the full-year guidance is increased (they now see sales of EU36-40 B vs. the Street EU34-39 B) while mgmt. expresses optimism on the macro landscape (“Demand for chips is outpacing supply. In response, our customers are accelerating their capacity expansion plans for 2026 and beyond”), although the Q2 forecast fell a bit short (they see sales of EU8.4-9 B vs. the Street EU9.05 B) ( ASML ); Hermes shares slump in European trading after the company reported a shortfall in Q1 sales growth (+5.6% on an FXN basis vs. the Street forecast of +7.4%), with weakness in Leather (+9.4% vs. the Street +10.8%), Watches (-3.7% vs. the Street +2.8%), Ready-to-wear and Accessories (+0.4% vs. the Street +6%), Asia (+3.5% vs. the Street +6.1%), and France (-2.8% vs. the Street +2.4%) ( Hermes ); Hotels/World Cup : US hotel operators are slashing room rates for the summer as World Cup demand falls short of expectations ( FT ); Kering shares slide in European trading after the company reported a modest shortfall in overall sales (organic revenue was flat vs. the Street forecast of +1%), w/weakness in Gucci (-8% organic) ( Kering ); Stellantis shares rally in Europe after the company reported solid Q1 shipment growth (+12% to 1.4 M units, w/strength in North America at +17%, Asia Pacific at +15%, and Europe at +12%) ( Stellantis ); UBER (Uber) is spending aggressively to build its robotaxi business, committing more than $10 B to buy thousands of vehicles and take equity stakes in their developers ( FT )
• Market in a Minute (micro – Tuesday night) - Anthropic has received “multiple offers” from VC firms looking to invest at a valuation of as much as $800 B (this is more than double the $380 B valuation secured during the last funding round in Feb) ( Business Insider ); Anthropic is increasing prices for heavy Claude users as the company grapples with a severe capacity shortage ( The Information ); AVGO (Broadcom) announced an extended partnership w/Meta to support the deployment of Meta’s MTIA (Meta Training and Inference Accelerator) chip. The initial commitment exceeds 1 GW and is the first phase of a sustained, multi-gigawatt rollout ( Broadcom ); data center prohibitions : Maine lawmakers passed a bill that would freeze approvals for data centers needing >20 MW of power until Oct 2027 ( Reuters ); NKE (Nike) saw some insider buying, including CEO Elliott Hill (who purchased 23.6 K shares) and board member (and Apple CEO) Tim Cook (who bought 25 K shares); Open AI will adopt a Mythos-like approach for its next AI model, releasing it early to a small group of users to ensure the tech industry overall is prepared for its cybersecurity capabilities ( Bloomberg , FT ); Space X plans to hold its IPO road show in early June, followed by an event for retail investors on June 11 ( Reuters )
• Calendar for Wednesday 4/15 – the focus will be on earnings before the US open ( BAC, PGR, PNC, MS, MTB ), the US Empire Manufacturing Index for Apr (8:30am ET), US import/export prices for Mar (8:30am ET), the NAHB housing market index for Apr (10am ET), the Fed’s Beige Book (2pm ET), earnings after the US close ( HOMB, SLG ), China’s new home prices for Mar (Wed night), Australia’s jobs report for Mar (Wed night), and China’s retail sales/industrial production for Mar and GDP for Q1 (Wed night).
• Calendar for Thursday 4/16 – the focus will be on earnings before the EU open ( Entain, Pernod Ricard, Rentokil, Tesco ), UK GDP and industrial/manufacturing production for Feb (2am ET), earnings before the US open ( ABT, BK, CFG, KEY, MRSH, PEP, PLD, SCHW, TRV, TSM, USB ), US weekly jobless claims (8:30am ET), the Philadelphia Fed index for April (8:30am ET), US industrial/manufacturing production for March (9:15am ET), and earnings after the US close ( AA, NFLX ).
• Calendar for Friday 4/17 – the focus will be on earnings before the EU open ( Ericsson ), the Eurozone trade balance for Feb (5am ET), and earnings before the US open ( ALLY, ALV, FITB, RF, STT, TFC ).

Iran

• What bulls are saying : media reports suggest the US and Iran remain in communication and are working to narrow their differences , while another face-to-face meeting could occur within days . Trump told Fox’s Maria Bartiromo in an interview airing Wed morning that the war is “very close to being over” and mentioned to ABC News that “ I think you’re going to be watching an amazing two days ahead ” while saying in a NY Post interview that additional peace negotiations “could be happening over the next two days” . It appears that Trump has given up on kinetic escalation as a viable option. A Bloomberg report said Iran could suspend Hormuz shipments for the time being to avoid risking a military confrontation with the US blockade. Europe is putting together a plan that would see it commit military resources to clearing Hormuz of mines once fighting formally ends according to the WSJ . Israel and Lebanon have a long way to go before the two sides reach a deal, but the fact they are even sitting down (as they did on Tuesday) is significant (the US State Dept. issued a statement Tues afternoon and expressed hope that the current dialogue could eventually yield an agreement ).
• What bears are saying : Trump remains stuck in the Middle East , unable to escalate or exit . The bid-ask between Washington and Tehran still seems wide (20 vs. 5 years on enrichment suspension is a big gap), and time is of the essence with the ceasefire set to expire next week. A Washington Post report Wed morning talked about how the Pentagon is moving thousands of additional troops into the Middle East . It’s not just Iran – the Lebanon war continues to rage and resolving that will be even harder than reconciling the nuclear differences between Washington and Tehran. The economic fallout from the war is only just starting to seep into the data , and investors should be prepared for months (at least) of stagflationary pressure (the Mar PPI wasn’t as hot as feared, but headline still accelerated meaningfully vs. Feb to +4% Y/Y).
• Iran would need to significantly reduce oil production within the next 2 weeks if the US blockade continues ( FT )
• Iran acquired a Chinese space satellite and used it to target US military bases in the Middle East ( FT )

International macro

• Bank of Korea’s incoming chief makes hawkish remarks on inflation risks given Iran war ( Reuters )
• Canada will temporarily suspend its gas tax to provide relief to consumers amid fallout from the Iran war ( NYT )
• China could impose limits on exports to the US of equipment to manufacturer solar panels ( Reuters )
• Cuba accused by the White House of aiding Russia’s war in Ukraine ( Axios )
• Europe is preparing a NATO replacement in case Trump winds up exiting the US from the alliance ( WSJ )
• Russia’s oil revenue nearly doubled in March thanks to the spike in global prices ( NYT )

US macro/Washington

• Yellen said the Fed could still cut rates later this year despite fallout from the Iran war ( Bloomberg )
• Yellen accuses Trump’s pressure campaign on the Fed as being like a “ banana republic ” ( FT )
• Prosecutors from Pirro’s office appeared at a Fed building on Tues but were turned away ( Washington Post )
• The Mar PPI (out Tues morning) wasn’t nearly as bad as feared , w/ headline coming in at +4% (vs. the Street +4.6% and vs. +3.4% in Feb) while core was +3.6% (vs. the Street +3.8% and vs. +3.5% in Feb).
• The Cass monthly freight volume index (out Tues morning) came in ahead of expectations/seasonality in Mar (North America shipment volumes rose 3% M/M, beating the seasonal expectation by 1%).
• The latest IMF economic report (which was published Tues morning and can be found here ) included reduced growth forecasts and higher inflation estimates , and warned of significant downside risks facing the global economy due to the Middle East war .
• Bessent on Tues said US tariffs could be back to where they stood before the SCOTUS decision by July ( Bloomberg )
• The US gov’t on Tues said it would launch its tariff refund system this Monday.
• NY Gov Hochul has proposed an annual tax on second homes in NYC worth $5 M+ ( NYT )
• White House leaning toward Erica Schwartz to run the CDC ( WSJ )

Autos

• Stellantis shares rally in Europe after the company reported solid Q1 shipment growth (+12% to 1.4 M units, w/strength in North America at +17%, Asia Pacific at +15%, and Europe at +12%) ( Stellantis )

Consumer

• Hermes shares slump in European trading after the company reported a shortfall in Q1 sales growth (+5.6% on an FXN basis vs. the Street forecast of +7.4%), with weakness in Leather (+9.4% vs. the Street +10.8%), Watches (-3.7% vs. the Street +2.8%), Ready-to-wear and Accessories (+0.4% vs. the Street +6%), Asia (+3.5% vs. the Street +6.1%), and France (-2.8% vs. the Street +2.4%) ( Hermes )
• Hotels/World Cup : US hotel operators are slashing room rates for the summer as World Cup demand falls short of expectations ( FT )
• Kering shares slide in European trading after the company reported a modest shortfall in overall sales (organic revenue was flat vs. the Street forecast of +1%), w/ weakness in Gucci (-8% organic) but strength in other brands (including YSL, Bottega, Balenciaga, and Brioni), along with Jewelry (+22%) and Eyewear (+7%) ( Kering )
• NKE (Nike) saw some insider buying , including CEO Elliott Hill (who purchased 23.6 K shares) and board member (and Apple CEO) Tim Cook (who bought 25 K shares).
• UAL (United)/AAL (American) – any attempt by the companies to merge would face fierce pushback from a whole host of regulatory agencies and constituencies ( Reuters )

Financials

• Aegon will sell its UK insurance business to Standard Life for GBP2 B ( WSJ )
• ARES (Ares)’s co-founder and CEO said credit quality is relatively healthy in the private credit industry ( Reuters )
• Bank earnings were (mostly) decent on Tues, especially JPM and Citigroup , and the macro message from bank mgmt. teams was encouraging despite all the recent volatility ( Jamie Dimon said “the U.S. economy remained resilient in the quarter, with consumers still earning and spending and businesses still healthy” while Charlie Scharf noted that “we still see continued resiliency in the underlying economy and the financial health of the consumers and businesses we serve remains strong”).

Industrials

• BA (Boeing) reported 46 deliveries in Mar , down from 51 in Feb due to the previously disclosed 737 MAX wiring issue ( Reuters )
• China chemical makers idle production amid surging input costs and soft demand ( Bloomberg )

M&A/Strategic Actions

• Aegon will sell its UK insurance business to Standard Life for GBP2 B ( WSJ )
• Kraken has filed confidentially for an IPO ( Semafor )
• Space X plans to hold its IPO road show in early June, followed by an event for retail investors on June 11 ( Reuters )
• UAL (United)/AAL (American) – any attempt by the companies to merge would face fierce pushback from a whole host of regulatory agencies and constituencies ( Reuters )

TMT

• Aixtron shares spike in European trading after the company preannounced upside on Q1 orders (EU171 MM vs. the Street EU134 MM) and raised its full-year guidance for sales and EBIT margins due to stronger than expected optoelectronics equipment demand ( Aixtron )
• Anthropic has received “multiple offers” from VC firms looking to invest at a valuation of as much as $800 B (this is more than double the $380 B valuation secured during the last funding round in Feb) ( Business Insider )
• Anthropic is increasing prices for heavy Claude users as the company grapples with a severe capacity shortage ( The Information )
• Anthropic’s next flagship model , Claude Opus 4.7 , will include new AI tools for designing websites and presentations according to The Information on Tues, news that hurt stocks like ADBE, FIG, GDDY , and WIX ( The Information )
• ASML (ASML Holding) reported solid upside on Q1 EPS (EU7.15 vs. the Street EU6.65) thanks to strength in sales and margins , and the full-year guidance is increased (they now see sales of EU36-40 B vs. the Street EU34-39 B) while mgmt. expresses optimism on the macro landscape (“Demand for chips is outpacing supply. In response, our customers are accelerating their capacity expansion plans for 2026 and beyond”), although the Q2 forecast fell a bit short (they see sales of EU8.4-9 B vs. the Street EU9.05 B) ( ASML )
• Data center prohibitions : Maine lawmakers passed a bill that would freeze approvals for data centers needing >20 MW of power until Oct 2027 ( Reuters )
• AVGO (Broadcom) announced an extended partnership w/Meta to support the deployment of Meta’s MTIA (Meta Training and Inference Accelerator) chip . The initial commitment exceeds 1 GW and is the first phase of a sustained, multi-gigawatt rollout ( Broadcom )
• CMCSA (Comcast)’s NBCUniversal says Nielsen is overstating streaming viewership and hurting legacy media companies in the process ( WSJ )
• GTLB (Git Lab) announced an expanded collaboration with Google Cloud (Google Cloud customers can now power Git Lab Duo Agent Platform with the Vertex AI models they already use, and count that usage toward their existing Google Cloud commitments)
• LUMN (Lumen) says more than half of all internet traffic is now made up of AI bots ( Bloomberg )
• Open AI will adopt a Mythos-like approach for its next AI model, releasing it early to a small group of users to ensure the tech industry overall is prepared for its cybersecurity capabilities ( Bloomberg , FT )
• Satellite vs. terrestrial : while Leo and Starlink are both positioning themselves as compliments to existing terrestrial cellular networks , it seems inevitable the two industries will become larger competitors going forward , and it wouldn’t be shocking to see Amazon sell a device in the future that provides complete voice/data coverage on Leo .
• Smartphones – the overall industry saw a Y/Y shipment decline in Q1 due to surging memory prices and macro uncertainty (although Apple and Samsung both posted small Y/Y increases ) ( Bloomberg )
• Space X plans to hold its IPO road show in early June, followed by an event for retail investors on June 11 ( Reuters )
• UBER (Uber) is spending aggressively to build its robotaxi business , committing more than $10 B to buy thousands of vehicles and take equity stakes in their developers ( FT )
• UPS (United Parcel Service) is aggressively rolling out RFID tracking technology to its logistics network ( WSJ )
• x AI was sued by the NAACP over the gas turbines powering a massive data center in Mississippi ( Barron’s )

Calendar for the week of Monday April 20

• What to watch during the week of Monday 4/20 – the key macro events this week include US retail sales for Mar (Tues 4/21 at 8:30am ET), Warsh’s Fed confirmation hearing (Tues 4/21), and the US flash PMIs for April (Thurs 4/23 at 9:45am ET). The Fed will be in a quiet period ahead of its decision on 4/29 . The key US earnings reports include: Mon morning ( CLF ); Mon night ( STLD and ZION ); Tues morning ( DGX, DHI, DHR, GE, HAL, MMM, NOC, RTX , and UNH ); Tues night ( CB, COF, ISRG , and UAL ); Wed morning ( BA, BSX, ELV, MAS, MCO, OTIS, PM, T, TEL , and VRT ); Wed night ( CCI, CSX, IBM, KNX, LRCX, LUV, NOW, TSLA, TXN , and URI ); Thurs morning ( AAL, AXP, BX, CMCSA, DOW, FCX, HON, KDP, KMT, PHM, TMO , and UNP ); Thurs night ( BKR, INTC, NEM , and SAP ); and Fri morning ( CHTR, HCA, NSC, PG , and SLB ). For European earnings , the key reports include: Wed morning ( ABB, Akzo Nobel, Danone , and Reckitt Benckiser ) and Thurs morning ( BE Semiconductor, Heineken, LSEG, Man Group, Nestle, Nokia, Roche, Sanofi, Schindler , and STM ).
• Calendar for Sunday 4/19 – the focus will be on China’s 1/5-year Loan Prime Rate decision (Sunday night).

vital.thinking_about_markets:
How we’re thinking about markets – we continue to feel sanguine about the durability of the current Iran ceasefire (it’s likely the détente gets extended , and the two sides eventually reach some type of a sustainable understanding ) and Q1 earnings have been impressive so far (although it’s still very early in the reporting period). However, our Iran optimism is no longer that far out of the consensus (although there remains a fair degree of skepticism and cynicism), there is still going to be economic fallout from the war even if the fighting permanently ends, and conditions won’t go back to the way they were for a long time (meaning that ~$90 could be a Brent floor into the summer, if not beyond). In addition, the SPX has surged ~10% from its ~6300 trough in pretty much a straight line (the nadir was reached on 3/30), the index is within the 6900-7000 pre-war resistance zone , and technical tailwinds (in terms of positioning and sentiment) have diminished (but people are still pretty negative – a Bo A survey found that fund managers are the most bearish they’ve been since Q2 of last year). Because of this, we’re turning neutral in the near-term .

vital.iran_section:
Iran . Trump sprayed the news wires with a lot of hopeful rhetoric during a trio of interviews on Tues (NY Post, Fox, and ABC) and myriad media outlets are talking about another face-to-face meeting within days, but (so far) nothing formal has been scheduled. It’s slowly becoming the consensus that the war (at least the military component of it) has peaked , with a sustained détente now the base case assumption (that’s our perspective too, but the removal of skepticism creates a downside risk for markets to the extent any hiccups emerge ). A Washington Post article around 5am ET contained the somewhat ominous headline “US sends thousands more troops to Mideast” , although that framing is a bit misleading (the troops are on boats that have been traveling to the region for several days, and so this isn’t as incremental as it may seem ); all the tea leaves still suggest Trump is pivoting aggressively away from kinetic escalation . There wasn’t anything major on the economic data or monetary policy fronts since the Tues close. For earnings , the big developments are European luxury , where both Hermes and Kering are getting hit following underwhelming Q1 sales results, and European tech , where ASML and Aixtron rally on back of solid results/guidance.

Iran

• What bulls are saying : media reports suggest the US and Iran remain in communication and are working to narrow their differences , while another face-to-face meeting could occur within days . Trump told Fox’s Maria Bartiromo in an interview airing Wed morning that the war is “very close to being over” and mentioned to ABC News that “ I think you’re going to be watching an amazing two days ahead ” while saying in a NY Post interview that additional peace negotiations “could be happening over the next two days” . It appears that Trump has given up on kinetic escalation as a viable option. A Bloomberg report said Iran could suspend Hormuz shipments for the time being to avoid risking a military confrontation with the US blockade. Europe is putting together a plan that would see it commit military resources to clearing Hormuz of mines once fighting formally ends according to the WSJ . Israel and Lebanon have a long way to go before the two sides reach a deal, but the fact they are even sitting down (as they did on Tuesday) is significant (the US State Dept. issued a statement Tues afternoon and expressed hope that the current dialogue could eventually yield an agreement ).
• What bears are saying : Trump remains stuck in the Middle East , unable to escalate or exit . The bid-ask between Washington and Tehran still seems wide (20 vs. 5 years on enrichment suspension is a big gap), and time is of the essence with the ceasefire set to expire next week. A Washington Post report Wed morning talked about how the Pentagon is moving thousands of additional troops into the Middle East . It’s not just Iran – the Lebanon war continues to rage and resolving that will be even harder than reconciling the nuclear differences between Washington and Tehran. The economic fallout from the war is only just starting to seep into the data , and investors should be prepared for months (at least) of stagflationary pressure (the Mar PPI wasn’t as hot as feared, but headline still accelerated meaningfully vs. Feb to +4% Y/Y).

• UAL (United)/AAL (American) – any attempt by the companies to merge would face fierce pushback from a whole host of regulatory agencies and constituencies ( Reuters )

Financials

• Aegon will sell its UK insurance business to Standard Life for GBP2 B ( WSJ )
• ARES (Ares)’s co-founder and CEO said credit quality is relatively healthy in the private credit industry ( Reuters )
• Bank earnings were (mostly) decent on Tues, especially JPM and Citigroup , and the macro message from bank mgmt. teams was encouraging despite all the recent volatility ( Jamie Dimon said “the U.S. economy remained resilient in the quarter, with consumers still earning and spending and businesses still healthy” while Charlie Scharf noted that “we still see continued resiliency in the underlying economy and the financial health of the consumers and businesses we serve remains strong”).

Industrials

• BA (Boeing) reported 46 deliveries in Mar , down from 51 in Feb due to the previously disclosed 737 MAX wiring issue ( Reuters )
• China chemical makers idle production amid surging input costs and soft demand ( Bloomberg )

M&A/Strategic Actions

• Aegon will sell its UK insurance business to Standard Life for GBP2 B ( WSJ )
• Kraken has filed confidentially for an IPO ( Semafor )
• Space X plans to hold its IPO road show in early June, followed by an event for retail investors on June 11 ( Reuters )
• UAL (United)/AAL (American) – any attempt by the companies to merge would face fierce pushback from a whole host of regulatory agencies and constituencies ( Reuters )

TMT

• Aixtron shares spike in European trading after the company preannounced upside on Q1 orders (EU171 MM vs. the Street EU134 MM) and raised its full-year guidance for sales and EBIT margins due to stronger than expected optoelectronics equipment demand ( Aixtron )
• Anthropic has received “multiple offers” from VC firms looking to invest at a valuation of as much as $800 B (this is more than double the $380 B valuation secured during the last funding round in Feb) ( Business Insider )
• Anthropic is increasing prices for heavy Claude users as the company grapples with a severe capacity shortage ( The Information )
• Anthropic’s next flagship model , Claude Opus 4.7 , will include new AI tools for designing websites and presentations according to The Information on Tues, news that hurt stocks like ADBE, FIG, GDDY , and WIX ( The Information )
• ASML (ASML Holding) reported solid upside on Q1 EPS (EU7.15 vs. the Street EU6.65) thanks to strength in sales and margins , and the full-year guidance is increased (they now see sales of EU36-40 B vs. the Street EU34-39 B) while mgmt. expresses optimism on the macro landscape (“Demand for chips is outpacing supply. In response, our customers are accelerating their capacity expansion plans for 2026 and beyond”), although the Q2 forecast fell a bit short (they see sales of EU8.4-9 B vs. the Street EU9.05 B) ( ASML )
• Data center prohibitions : Maine lawmakers passed a bill that would freeze approvals for data centers needing >20 MW of power until Oct 2027 ( Reuters )
• AVGO (Broadcom) announced an extended partnership w/Meta to support the deployment of Meta’s MTIA (Meta Training and Inference Accelerator) chip . The initial commitment exceeds 1 GW and is the first phase of a sustained, multi-gigawatt rollout ( Broadcom )
• CMCSA (Comcast)’s NBCUniversal says Nielsen is overstating streaming viewership and hurting legacy media companies in the process ( WSJ )
• GTLB (Git Lab) announced an expanded collaboration with Google Cloud (Google Cloud customers can now power Git Lab Duo Agent Platform with the Vertex AI models they already use, and count that usage toward their existing Google Cloud commitments)
• LUMN (Lumen) says more than half of all internet traffic is now made up of AI bots ( Bloomberg )
• Open AI will adopt a Mythos-like approach for its next AI model, releasing it early to a small group of users to ensure the tech industry overall is prepared for its cybersecurity capabilities ( Bloomberg , FT )
• Satellite vs. terrestrial : while Leo and Starlink are both positioning themselves as compliments to existing terrestrial cellular networks , it seems inevitable the two industries will become larger competitors going forward , and it wouldn’t be shocking to see Amazon sell a device in the future that provides complete voice/data coverage on Leo .
• Smartphones – the overall industry saw a Y/Y shipment decline in Q1 due to surging memory prices and macro uncertainty (although Apple and Samsung both posted small Y/Y increases ) ( Bloomberg )
• Space X plans to hold its IPO road show in early June, followed by an event for retail investors on June 11 ( Reuters )
• UBER (Uber) is spending aggressively to build its robotaxi business , committing more than $10 B to buy thousands of vehicles and take equity stakes in their developers ( FT )
• UPS (United Parcel Service) is aggressively rolling out RFID tracking technology to its logistics network ( WSJ )

energy, telecoms, insurance, banks, and luxury underperform . Aixtron and ASML are both climbing on earnings ( Aixton is the top stock in the SXXP) while Hermes and Kering get hit hard following their results. Stellantis is climbing on solid Q1 shipment numbers. Stocks in Asia mostly saw gains on Wed: TPX +0.4%, NKY +0.44%, Hang Seng +0.29%, HSCEI +0.54%, SHCOMP +0.01%, Shenzhen -0.71%, Taiwan TAIEX +1.17%, Korea KOSPI +2.07%, Australia ASX 200 +0.09%, and India is up ~1.6%. Treasuries are seeing modest weakness so far this morning (yields are flat-to-up 2bp) and Fed expectations aren’t moving much (the market is pricing in 10bp of Fed cuts this year). The DXY is about flat. Brent is climbing 60bp to ~$95.35 while gold falls 75bp and silver drops 90bp. Bitcoin is inching down 15bp (to ~$74 K).
• What’s happening this morning – it’s fairly quiet as markets wait for the next development out of Iran . Trump sprayed the news wires with a lot of hopeful rhetoric during a trio of interviews on Tues (NY Post, Fox, and ABC) and myriad media outlets are talking about another face-to-face meeting within days, but (so far) nothing formal has been scheduled. It’s slowly becoming the consensus that the war (at least the military component of it) has peaked , with a sustained détente now the base case assumption (that’s our perspective too, but the removal of skepticism creates a downside risk for markets to the extent any hiccups emerge ). A Washington Post article around 5am ET contained the somewhat ominous headline “US sends thousands more troops to Mideast” , although that framing is a bit misleading (the troops are on boats that have been traveling to the region for several days, and so this isn’t as incremental as it may seem ); all the tea leaves still suggest Trump is pivoting aggressively away from kinetic escalation . There wasn’t anything major on the economic data or monetary policy fronts since the Tues close. For earnings , the big developments are European luxury , where both Hermes and Kering are getting hit following underwhelming Q1 sales results, and European tech , where ASML and Aixtron rally on back of solid results/guidance.
• How we’re thinking about markets – we continue to feel sanguine about the durability of the current Iran ceasefire (it’s likely the détente gets extended , and the two sides eventually reach some type of a sustainable understanding ) and Q1 earnings have been impressive so far (although it’s still very early in the reporting period). However, our Iran optimism is no longer that far out of the consensus (although there remains a fair degree of skepticism and cynicism), there is still going to be economic fallout from the war even if the fighting permanently ends, and conditions won’t go back to the way they were for a long time (meaning that ~$90 could be a Brent floor into the summer, if not beyond). In addition, the SPX has surged ~10% from its ~6300 trough in pretty much a straight line (the nadir was reached on 3/30), the index is within the 6900-7000 pre-war resistance zone , and technical tailwinds (in terms of positioning and sentiment) have diminished (but people are still pretty negative – a Bo A survey found that fund managers are the most bearish they’ve been since Q2 of last year). Because of this, we’re turning neutral in the near-term .

• Calendar for Friday 4/17 – the focus will be on earnings before the EU open ( Ericsson ), the Eurozone trade balance for Feb (5am ET), and earnings before the US open ( ALLY, ALV, FITB, RF, STT, TFC ).

Iran

• What bulls are saying : media reports suggest the US and Iran remain in communication and are working to narrow their differences , while another face-to-face meeting could occur within days . Trump told Fox’s Maria Bartiromo in an interview airing Wed morning that the war is “very close to being over” and mentioned to ABC News that “ I think you’re going to be watching an amazing two days ahead ” while saying in a NY Post interview that additional peace negotiations “could be happening over the next two days” . It appears that Trump has given up on kinetic escalation as a viable option. A Bloomberg report said Iran could suspend Hormuz shipments for the time being to avoid risking a military confrontation with the US blockade. Europe is putting together a plan that would see it commit military resources to clearing Hormuz of mines once fighting formally ends according to the WSJ . Israel and Lebanon have a long way to go before the two sides reach a deal, but the fact they are even sitting down (as they did on Tuesday) is significant (the US State Dept. issued a statement Tues afternoon and expressed hope that the current dialogue could eventually yield an agreement ).
• What bears are saying : Trump remains stuck in the Middle East , unable to escalate or exit . The bid-ask between Washington and Tehran still seems wide (20 vs. 5 years on enrichment suspension is a big gap), and time is of the essence with the ceasefire set to expire next week. A Washington Post report Wed morning talked about how the Pentagon is moving thousands of additional troops into the Middle East . It’s not just Iran – the Lebanon war continues to rage and resolving that will be even harder than reconciling the nuclear differences between Washington and Tehran. The economic fallout from the war is only just starting to seep into the data , and investors should be prepared for months (at least) of stagflationary pressure (the Mar PPI wasn’t as hot as feared, but headline still accelerated meaningfully vs. Feb to +4% Y/Y).
• Iran would need to significantly reduce oil production within the next 2 weeks if the US blockade continues ( FT )
• Iran acquired a Chinese space satellite and used it to target US military bases in the Middle East ( FT )

International macro

• Bank of Korea’s incoming chief makes hawkish remarks on inflation risks given Iran war ( Reuters )
• Canada will temporarily suspend its gas tax to provide relief to consumers amid fallout from the Iran war ( NYT )
• China could impose limits on exports to the US of equipment to manufacturer solar panels ( Reuters )
• Cuba accused by the White House of aiding Russia’s war in Ukraine ( Axios )
• Europe is preparing a NATO replacement in case Trump winds up exiting the US from the alliance ( WSJ )
• Russia’s oil revenue nearly doubled in March thanks to the spike in global prices ( NYT )

US macro/Washington

• Yellen said the Fed could still cut rates later this year despite fallout from the Iran war ( Bloomberg )
• Yellen accuses Trump’s pressure campaign on the Fed as being like a “ banana republic ” ( FT )
• Prosecutors from Pirro’s office appeared at a Fed building on Tues but were turned away ( Washington Post )
• The Mar PPI (out Tues morning) wasn’t nearly as bad as feared , w/ headline coming in at +4% (vs. the Street +4.6% and vs. +3.4% in Feb) while core was +3.6% (vs. the Street +3.8% and vs. +3.5% in Feb).
• The Cass monthly freight volume index (out Tues morning) came in ahead of expectations/seasonality in Mar (North America shipment volumes rose 3% M/M, beating the seasonal expectation by 1%).
• The latest IMF economic report (which was published Tues morning and can be found here ) included reduced growth forecasts and higher inflation estimates , and warned of significant downside risks facing the global economy due to the Middle East war .
• Bessent on Tues said US tariffs could be back to where they stood before the SCOTUS decision by July ( Bloomberg )
• The US gov’t on Tues said it would launch its tariff refund system this Monday.
• NY Gov Hochul has proposed an annual tax on second homes in NYC worth $5 M+ ( NYT )
• White House leaning toward Erica Schwartz to run the CDC ( WSJ )

Autos

• Stellantis shares rally in Europe after the company reported solid Q1 shipment growth (+12% to 1.4 M units, w/strength in North America at +17%, Asia Pacific at +15%, and Europe at +12%) ( Stellantis )

Consumer

• Hermes shares slump in European trading after the company reported a shortfall in Q1 sales growth (+5.6% on an FXN basis vs. the Street forecast of +7.4%), with weakness in Leather (+9.4% vs. the Street +10.8%), Watches (-3.7% vs. the Street +2.8%), Ready-to-wear and Accessories (+0.4% vs. the Street +6%), Asia (+3.5% vs. the Street +6.1%), and France (-2.8% vs. the Street +2.4%) ( Hermes )
• Hotels/World Cup : US hotel operators are slashing room rates for the summer as World Cup demand falls short of expectations ( FT )
• Kering shares slide in European trading after the company reported a modest shortfall in overall sales (organic revenue was flat vs. the Street forecast of +1%), w/ weakness in Gucci (-8% organic) but strength in other brands (including YSL, Bottega, Balenciaga, and Brioni), along with Jewelry (+22%) and Eyewear (+7%) ( Kering )
• NKE (Nike) saw some insider buying , including CEO Elliott Hill (who purchased 23.6 K shares) and board member (and Apple CEO) Tim Cook (who bought 25 K shares).
• UAL (United)/AAL (American) – any attempt by the companies to merge would face fierce pushback from a whole host of regulatory agencies and constituencies ( Reuters )
vital.tmt_section:
TMT

• Aixtron shares spike in European trading after the company preannounced upside on Q1 orders (EU171 MM vs. the Street EU134 MM) and raised its full-year guidance for sales and EBIT margins due to stronger than expected optoelectronics equipment demand ( Aixtron )
• Anthropic has received “multiple offers” from VC firms looking to invest at a valuation of as much as $800 B (this is more than double the $380 B valuation secured during the last funding round in Feb) ( Business Insider )
• Anthropic is increasing prices for heavy Claude users as the company grapples with a severe capacity shortage ( The Information )
• Anthropic’s next flagship model , Claude Opus 4.7 , will include new AI tools for designing websites and presentations according to The Information on Tues, news that hurt stocks like ADBE, FIG, GDDY , and WIX ( The Information )
• ASML (ASML Holding) reported solid upside on Q1 EPS (EU7.15 vs. the Street EU6.65) thanks to strength in sales and margins , and the full-year guidance is increased (they now see sales of EU36-40 B vs. the Street EU34-39 B) while mgmt. expresses optimism on the macro landscape (“Demand for chips is outpacing supply. In response, our customers are accelerating their capacity expansion plans for 2026 and beyond”), although the Q2 forecast fell a bit short (they see sales of EU8.4-9 B vs. the Street EU9.05 B) ( ASML )
• Data center prohibitions : Maine lawmakers passed a bill that would freeze approvals for data centers needing >20 MW of power until Oct 2027 ( Reuters )
• AVGO (Broadcom) announced an extended partnership w/Meta to support the deployment of Meta’s MTIA (Meta Training and Inference Accelerator) chip . The initial commitment exceeds 1 GW and is the first phase of a sustained, multi-gigawatt rollout ( Broadcom )
• CMCSA (Comcast)’s NBCUniversal says Nielsen is overstating streaming viewership and hurting legacy media companies in the process ( WSJ )
• GTLB (Git Lab) announced an expanded collaboration with Google Cloud (Google Cloud customers can now power Git Lab Duo Agent Platform with the Vertex AI models they already use, and count that usage toward their existing Google Cloud commitments)
• LUMN (Lumen) says more than half of all internet traffic is now made up of AI bots ( Bloomberg )
• Open AI will adopt a Mythos-like approach for its next AI model, releasing it early to a small group of users to ensure the tech industry overall is prepared for its cybersecurity capabilities ( Bloomberg , FT )
• Satellite vs. terrestrial : while Leo and Starlink are both positioning themselves as compliments to existing terrestrial cellular networks , it seems inevitable the two industries will become larger competitors going forward , and it wouldn’t be shocking to see Amazon sell a device in the future that provides complete voice/data coverage on Leo .
• Smartphones – the overall industry saw a Y/Y shipment decline in Q1 due to surging memory prices and macro uncertainty (although Apple and Samsung both posted small Y/Y increases ) ( Bloomberg )
• Space X plans to hold its IPO road show in early June, followed by an event for retail investors on June 11 ( Reuters )
• UBER (Uber) is spending aggressively to build its robotaxi business , committing more than $10 B to buy thousands of vehicles and take equity stakes in their developers ( FT )
• UPS (United Parcel Service) is aggressively rolling out RFID tracking technology to its logistics network ( WSJ )
• x AI was sued by the NAACP over the gas turbines powering a massive data center in Mississippi ( Barron’s )

reuters.body_excerpt:
And, China seizes South China Sea entrance.

Daily Briefing

Daily Briefing

By Anisha De

Hello. Trump says talks with Iran could resume soon, China moves to block entrance to disputed South China Sea shoal and IMF warns world already drifting toward more adverse scenario as it cuts growth outlook .

Plus, Frenchman scoops up 1 million euro Picasso painting in Paris raffle.

Today's Top News

A vessel at the Strait of Hormuz, off the coast of Oman’s Musandam province, April 12, 2026. REUTERS/File Photo

War in the Middle East

•

The United States said its military had completely halted trade going in and out of Iran by sea , impacting 90% of the Iranian economy, while President Donald Trump said talks with Tehran on ending the war could resume this week, sending oil prices down for a second day.

• A US-sanctioned Chinese tanker Rich Starry made its way back to the Strait of Hormuz after exiting the Gulf the day before, shipping data ‌showed, failing to break through a US blockade on vessels calling at Iranian ports.

In other news

•

China is employing ships and a barrier to tighten control of the entrance to the Scarborough Shoal in the South China Sea amid roiling tension with the Philippines over the disputed feature, satellite imagery obtained by Reuters shows. Read our exclusive report .
•

Russia-linked hackers broke into more than 170 email accounts belonging to prosecutors and investigators across Ukraine during the last several months, according to data reviewed by Reuters, a campaign that shows how Moscow’s spies are keeping tabs on the Ukrainian officials tasked with rooting out corruption and Russian ‌collaborators.
• Democratic Republic of Congo is set to receive more than 30 deportees from the United States this week, four sources told Reuters, the latest example of Washington using agreements with African governments to accelerate migrant removals
•

Hungary's election winner Peter Magyar said that he will suspend state media ‌news broadcast s , which critics at home and abroad say became a government mouthpiece under Viktor Orban, and restore media freedoms after his cabinet takes power.

•

The Georgian government has announced higher education reforms , affecting funding and the geographical redistribution of faculties. Once seen in the West as a burgeoning democracy, on the fast track to joining the European Union, Georgia is increasingly turning its back on the West and deepening ties with Moscow.

Business & Markets

• The International Monetary Fund cut its growth outlook to 3.1% assuming a short-lived Iran war but said the world was already drifting toward a more adverse scenario of 2.5% growth in 2026 as Strait of Hormuz shipping disruptions continue.
• The US dollar has given back most of the gains sparked by the Iran war, as a tentative ceasefire revived appetite for riskier currencies, but investors said robust demand for US assets and waning prospects for US interest-rate cuts should buttress it against sharper declines.
•

Chinese officials have held initial talks with providers of equipment to make solar panels as they consider limiting exports of the most advanced technology to the United States, five people with knowledge of the consultations said.
•

Amazon.com said it will acquire Globalstar in an $11.57 billion deal , bolstering its fledgling satellite business as it tries to catch up with Elon Musk's Starlink. But Greg Bensinger tells the Reuters World News podcast the acquisition faces hurdles in rivaling Starlink because of a massive satellite count disparity. Listen now .

• The US Treasury's bank regulation agency has terminated the lease for the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau's Washington headquarters after 14 years and agreed to pass the premises to the federal government's general real estate management agency, records obtained by Reuters show.
•

Carmakers and battery companies facing a weak US market for electric vehicles are scrambling to repurpose battery factories to make energy-storage systems to fuel AI's thirst for power instead. But converting plants to new types of batteries won’t be easy, nor will the demand for energy storage materialize fast enough to absorb a glut of unused factory space for EV batteries.

• From DFS to Avolta, duty-free stores selling premium perfumes and spirits to big spenders are feeling the pinch as conflict in the Middle East shuts airports and curbs travel to the region, a setback likely to become more acute as ‌the war drags on.
•

India's markets regulator will allow companies to cut ‌the size of IPOs by as much as 50% without filing additional onerous paperwork as the Iran war has made it hard to follow through with initial plans, according to an email seen by Reuters.

Rattled by Trump, US allies eye Japan's biggest arms opening since World War Two

Type 10 tanks operate during a military drill by the Japanese Ground Self-Defense Force 1st Airborne Brigade in Japan, January 11, 2026. REUTERS/Kim Kyung-Hoon/File Ph
